meta.name: CAGE Alcohol Screening Questionnaire
meta.description: The CAGE is a widely used 4-item screening tool for identifying alcohol problems. The name is an acronym of its four questions: Cut down, Annoyed, Guilty, and Eye-opener. It is one of the most commonly used screening instruments in clinical practice.
meta.author: Ewing (1984)
meta.reference: Ewing JA. Detecting alcoholism: the CAGE questionnaire. JAMA. 1984;252(14):1905-1907.
meta.instruction: Please answer each question honestly with Yes or No.

questions.text: Have you ever felt you should Cut down on your drinking?
Have people Annoyed you by criticizing your drinking?
Have you ever felt bad or Guilty about your drinking?
Have you ever had a drink first thing in the morning to steady your nerves or to get rid of a hangover (Eye-opener)?

ranges.label: No Alcohol Problems Indicated
ranges.description: Your responses do not suggest alcohol-related problems. This indicates that your current substance-use-related risk is low and within a healthy range.
suggestions: Continue maintaining healthy drinking habits or abstinence
Stay informed about the risks of excessive alcohol use
Be mindful of any changes in your drinking patterns
Stay informed about risks related to substance use

ranges.label: Possible Alcohol Problem
ranges.description: Your response suggests a possible alcohol-related concern. Further evaluation may be helpful. A mild risk level suggests it may be helpful to pay attention to your usage patterns and maintain self-awareness.
suggestions: Reflect on your relationship with alcohol
Consider discussing your drinking habits with a healthcare provider
Monitor your drinking patterns over the coming weeks
Learn about recommended alcohol intake guidelines
Pay attention to your usage patterns and frequency

ranges.label: Probable Alcohol Problem
ranges.description: Your responses suggest a probable alcohol use problem. A score of 2 or more is clinically significant and warrants further assessment. Your score indicates elevated substance-use-related risk. Speaking with a professional for personalized support and guidance is recommended.
suggestions: Strongly recommended to seek professional evaluation for alcohol use
Consult with a healthcare provider or addiction specialist
Consider joining a support group for alcohol-related concerns
Talk openly with trusted family members or friends
Explore treatment options that may be available to you
